coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive diosmetin sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Chang
Tong, Rui
Deng, Guoqing
Yang, Jing
Zhang, Yi
Cai, Zhifeng
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a single signal fluorescent sensor of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) was established for the micro-determination of diosmetin. In this determination platform, the as-developed N-CQDs with yellow fluorescence were prepared through a facile hydrothermal method with p-phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid as the precursors containing nitrogen and carbon. The as-developed N-CQDs had a classical surface structure and surprising fluorescence stability. The maximum excitation and emission peaks were located at 372 and 528 nm, respectively. Under the optimal sensing conditions, the as-prepared N-CQDs held preeminent immunity towards possible interfering substances. The determination platform’s fluorescence intensity was quenched because of the static quenching and inner filter effect. The fluorescence weakening changes displayed an attractive linear relationship with diosmetin in range of 0.5–150 μM, and the linear equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.014[Q]–0.055 with the R2 of 0.9975 and the detection limit of 0.049 μM. The determination platform could be applied for the trace detection of diosmetin in river water and bovine serum samples. The corresponding recovery rate was 94.8 %–108.8 %, exhibiting that it was a facile and efficient fluorescence sensing strategy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence response
Diosmetin
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing of N-CQDs for diosmetin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image (A), size distribution (B), XRD patterns (C) and FT-IR spectrum (D) of N-CQDs.
The XPS spectra of N-CQDs (A), C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D).

The UV–vis (blank line), excitation (green line) and emission (blue line) spectra of N-CQDs (A); the emission spectra under different excitation wavelength (B). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs with different diosmetin concentration (A); the linear fitting curve (B).
The diosmetin detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive diosmetin sensing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a single signal fluorescent sensor of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) was established for the micro-determination of diosmetin. In this determination platform, the as-developed N-CQDs with yellow fluorescence were prepared through a facile hydrothermal method with p-phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid as the precursors containing nitrogen and carbon. The as-developed N-CQDs had a classical surface structure and surprising fluorescence stability. The maximum excitation and emission peaks were located at 372 and 528 nm, respectively. Under the optimal sensing conditions, the as-prepared N-CQDs held preeminent immunity towards possible interfering substances. The determination platform’s fluorescence intensity was quenched because of the static quenching and inner filter effect. The fluorescence weakening changes displayed an attractive linear relationship with diosmetin in range of 0.5–150 μM, and the linear equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.014[Q]–0.055 with the R2 of 0.9975 and the detection limit of 0.049 μM. The determination platform could be applied for the trace detection of diosmetin in river water and bovine serum samples. The corresponding recovery rate was 94.8 %–108.8 %, exhibiting that it was a facile and efficient fluorescence sensing strategy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Diosmetin (Fig. S1) is a flavonoid substance, and the chemical formula is C16H12O6 with relative molecular mass of 300 g/mol. Diosmetin is extracted from lemon and also found in many natural plants such as spearmint and spider fragrance [1,2]. Diosmetin is similar to other flavonoids. For example, diosmetin has antioxidant, anti-infective, and anti-shock effects and been used in some foods, cosmetics and drugs [3,4]. To our knowledge, the concentration determination of each substance is crucial. Thus, how to rapidly and sensitively determine diosmetin amount in real samples is a crux focus to be processed. To today, only UV Spectrophotometric technique and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) were used to detect the concentration of diosmetin [5]. Although these methods could afford high selectivity and sensitivity; but they had many drawbacks such as expensive instrumentation, high running costs, sample pre-treatment and long processing time. Recently, fluorescence determination and sensing strategy has carried out great interest due to its excellent sensitivity, facile process, low-priced and numerous detectable substances [6–9]. The core of fluorescence methods were the design and preparation of fluorescent materials. Up to now, the fluorescent materials included organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots and carbon quantum dots [10–13]. Among them, carbon quantum dots had many advantages, such as low toxicity, easy preparation and excellent optical properties.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a disruptive small-scale nanomaterial, were first developed in 2004. Some fascinating characteristics of CQDs were studied such as water solubility, photostability, optical properties, and biocompatibility [14–17]. Currently, the CQDs have been widely carried out by the researches. On the one hand, the CQDs could be used as photocatalysts to encourage the degradation of different pollutants through adjust the wavelength of light [18,19]. On the other hand, due to the biocompatibility and fluorescence performances, the CQDs were applied as the fluorescence nanomaterials in biomarkers and bioimaging, such as for the sensing of intracellular pH value and for the in vivo imaging of mice [20,21]. Fluorescence detection was also an important application field of CQDs. Some researchers utilized the fluorescence variation pattern of CQDs to quantitatively determine different substances, such as cations, anions, pH value and biological small molecules [22–26].

Recently, the studies about the determination of flavonoids using the CQDs have slowly developed. For example, Yan group developed ratiometric fluorescence probe based on B,N dual-doped carbon dots from sodium tetraborate and polyethyleneimine through a single-step hydrothermal method, and this probe was used to detect morin due to the static quenching mechanism [27]. Alkahtani et al. [28] prepared nitrogen doped carbon dots through a facile pyrolysis method from chicken feet biowaste and the fluorescence quantum yield could reach 42.9 %. After the addition of rutin, the fluorescence of the nitrogen doped carbon dots were quenched via inner filter effect and static quenching. The linear range and detection limit were 100–1000 nM and 5.3 nM, respectively. Li et al. developed a fluorescent nanosensor based on N, Cu-CDs for the visual discrimination of tetracyclines and quercetin. The fluorescence of N, Cu-CDs were weakened immediately because of the internal filtration effect [29]. It is thus clear that the CQDs could be applied for the determination of flavonoids. Nevertheless, the low fluorescence quantum yield for most CQDs was also an important limiting factor, which made it difficult to achieve the available utilization of the nanomaterial. Thus, the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs was an important attention for the fluorescence detection and biological imaging. Moreover, the doping of elements could not only change the surface state and electron distribution of the CQDs, but also improve the fluorescence yield of the CQDs, such as nitrogen doping, sulfur doping, and phosphorus doping [30–32]. Although the preparation mechanisms have not been clearly explained, the nitrogen-doped CQDs is considered one of the most interesting nanomaterial for the increasing of fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence detection range.

In this paper, we developed nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) via a one-pot hydrothermal strategy utilizing p-phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid as the precursors. The targets of this investigation were (a) to discuss the preparation programme of the N-CQDs; (b) to assess the dependability and sensitivity of the N-CQDs for the determination of diosmetin, including the development of the linear range, the study of the detection limits, the selective experiments and the applications in real samples; (c) to discuss the mechanism of the diosmetin for the N-CQDs; (d) to study the application in visual detection.

p-Phenylenediamine, ascorbic acid, arginine, glutathione, methionine, isoleucine, leucine, lysine, alanine, vancomycin, glucose, dithiothreitol, tyrosine, rosiglitazone, isoniazid, carbendazim, kanamycin, phloretin, glycine, amoxicillin, lincomycin, puerarin and diosmetin were Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise specified, the mentioned reagents are analytically pure without any purification. The relevant solutions were prepared with ultrapure water and N, N-dimethylformamide.

The fluorescence data of the N-CQDs with different diosmetin concentrations were researched via F-7000 molecular fluorescence analyzer (Hitachi, Japan) with the excitation/emission slits of 10 nm/10 nm. The UV–visible absorption spectral data of the N-CQDs were obtained utilizing UV-4500 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with 1 cm × 1 cm UV cuvette. The morphological features of the N-CQDs were investigated through JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) with the voltage of 200 kV. The image J software was used to discuss the particle size distribution of the N-CQDs. The chemical composition and oxidation state of N-CQDs were studied using Axis-Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, Shimadzu, Japan). The crystal form of the N-CQDs was carried out using the Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, Japan) with the range of 10-80°. The types of functional groups on the N-CQDs surface were discussed via Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, America) with the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The EI fls1000 (Britain) was applied for the measurement of the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs.

The project design was developed by referring to relevant literature [33,34]. p-Phenylenediamine of 0.2 g and ascorbic acid of 0.1 g were added successively into 100 mL beaker containing 30 mL ultrapure water with stirring for 20 min at room temperature. The solution gradually became clear and was introduced into a 50 mL Teflon hydrothermal kettle. The hydrothermal kettle was placed into the Drying box for 6 h under 180 ℃. After the completion of hydrothermal reaction, the obtained solution was cooled naturally to 25 ℃ and filtered via the filter paper. The filtered solution was dialyzed by using dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da). Finally, the N-CQDs powders were obtained through drying the dialyzed solution.

The fluorescence quantum yield was measured by referencing published articles [35,36]. The quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.05 M H2SO4, with a quantum yield of 0.54) was selected as the reference. The corresponding calculation formula was as follows:
(1)
Q =
Q S
×
A S
/
A X
×
I X
/
I S
×
η X
/
η S
2

where Q refers to quantum yield, A stands for the absorbance under the excitation wavelength, I represents the emission fluorescence intensity, η stands for the solvent refraction index. S and X stand for quinine sulfate and N-CQDs, respectively. The Q value of the N-CQDs was calculated to be 19.7 %.

The citric acid and sodium citrate buffer solution (pH = 6) was applied for the dilution of the N-CQDs (200 μL raw solution and 1.8 mL buffer solution). The selective experimental solutions were prepared by different control substances, such as arginine, glutathione, methionine, isoleucine, leucine, lysine, alanine, vancomycin, glucose, dithiothreitol, tyrosine, rosiglitazone, isoniazid, carbendazim, kanamycin, phloretin, glycine, amoxicillin, lincomycin, puerarin and diosmetin. Then, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was used to estimate the selectivity of N-CQDs towards diosmetin based on the measured fluorescence data.

To evaluate the feasibility of N-CQDs for diosmetin, we conducted some experiments in real samples, such as river water and bovine serum samples. All real samples were filtered via filter paper and diluted with citric acid and sodium citrate buffer solution. Then the diosmetin solutions were spiked into the processed actual samples. The actual concentrations of diosmetin were obtained through measured fluorescence intensities.

TEM measurement displayed that the morphology of N-CQDs was spherical and high dispersion (Fig. 1 A). As exhibited in Fig. 1B, the average diameter was 2.5 ± 0.02 nm by counting 100 nanoparticles. A significant diffraction peak was observed in Fig. 1C (the XRD pattern of N-CQDs), indicating the amorphous structure of N-CQDs, which was consistent with the existing carbon quantum dots [37]. The types of functional groups of N-CQDs were studied by FT-IR and the spectra were displayed in Fig. 1D. The stretching vibration of O-H or N-H bond was located at 3451.5 cm−1 [38]. The broad spike peak near 1632.5 cm−1 was considered as the stretching vibration of carbonyl [39]. The peaks at 2969.8 cm−1 and 2912.8 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of carbon-hydrogen bond [40]. The stretching vibration of carbon–oxygen bond was located at 1119.5 cm−1, respectively [41]. The feature peaks at 1354.7 cm−1 and 762.2 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C-C and C-N bonds [42]. The FT-IR results indicated that the N-CQDs have been successfully developed and the nitrogen has been successfully introduced into the surface of N-CQDs.

The XPS analysis was applied to discuss the element composition, content and oxidation state of the N-CQDs. As indicated in Fig. 2 A, the C, N and O were located at 285.4 eV, 401.7 eV and 532.6 eV, and the relative contents of C, N and O were 52.61 %, 26.12 % and 21.27 %, respectively. The single spectrum of carbon was divided into three peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.1 eV, which were assigned to C = C, C-N and C = O bonds, respectively [43] (Fig. 2B). In single spectrum of nitrogen, three feature peaks at 398.4 eV, 400.1 eV and 401.1 eV were observed, which came from the separation of XPS spectrum of nitrogen [44] (Fig. 2C). The single XPS spectrum of oxygen was divided into three peaks at 530.1 eV, 531.7 eV and 532.6 eV, which were ascribed to O-C-O, C-OH and C = O bonds, respectively [45] (Fig. 2D). Thus, the XPS results were consistent with FT-IR results, exhibiting the successful preparation of the N-CQDs.

In UV–vis spectrum of N-CQDs, two peaks at 305 nm and 512 nm were observed (Fig. 3 A). The peak at 305 nm was ascribed to the π-π* transition of conjugated C = C bond, the peak at 512 nm was assigned to the n-π* transition of C = O bond [46]. The color of N-CQDs was studied through fluorescent dark box. As displayed in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence color of N-CQDs displayed yellow. Meanwhile, the N-CQDs solution indicated light purple under sunlight. When the optimal excitation wavelength was 372 nm, the emission wavelength was 528 nm. Moreover, we studied the influence of excitation wavelength on the emission wavelength. As exhibited in Fig. 3B, the emission wavelength did not have a fixed value under different excitation wavelength, suggesting the excitation-dependent performance of emission wavelength.

Next, we discussed the influence of different conditions on the stability of N-CQDs, such as storage time, UV light exposure time, sodium chloride and pH value. In Fig. S2, the N-CQDs displayed surprising stability at 4 ℃ and 25 ℃. After 2 min of irradiation, the fluorescence suggested a slight decrease but could be ignored, exhibiting the excellent photostability of N-CQDs (Fig. S3). When the NaCl concentration reached 0.05 M in N-CQDs solution, there was a slight increase in fluorescence, indicating the significant tolerance in salt solution (Fig. S4). In Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity would significantly decrease under over-acidic or over-alkaline conditions and reached maximum value under neutral conditions. The fluorescence intensity dropped significantly in extreme acidic or basic pH that could be ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation significantly of the surface functional groups [47,48]. The excellent stability of N-CQDs was beneficial for wider applications in many fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of N-CQDs for diosmetin
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the N-CQDs were developed as fluorescence nanosensors for the measurement of diosmetin. After the addition of diosmetin, the fluorescence intensity could be quenched under the detection conditions.

When the diosmetin amounts changing from 0 to 150 μM in the N-CQDs solution, the fluorescent intensities at 528 nm were carried out under the excitation wavelength of 372 nm (Fig. 4 A). The height of the peak gradually decreased. A surprising linear relationship has been discovered by fitting the relationship between ln(F0/F) and the diosmetin amounts (changing from 0.5 μM to 150 μM). The linear fitting equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.014[Q]-0.055 with the fitting coefficient of 0.9975 (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.049 μM according to the calculation results of formula LOD = 3b/k. Compared with existing literature reports for diosmetin sensing, the linear determination range and LOD of the N-CQDs based fluorescence sensor of diosmetin were equivalent and even superior to the existing literature reports (Table S1).

The selectivity of N-CQDs for the sensing of diosmetin was investigated under the same conditions. Even if the concentrations of control substances were 10 times that of diosmetin, the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs did not significantly decrease (Fig. S6). Moreover, the control substances were added into the N-CQDs + diosmetin mixture. The recovery of fluorescence intensities have not been discovered based on the above results, indicating that the determination procedure of diosmetin would not be disturbed by these control substances. So, this platform based on N-CQDs was a selectively fluorescent nanosensor for diosmetin determination. The N-CQDs possessed an extensive application foreground in real samples.

The practicability of fluorescent nanosensor in real samples limited their application foreground. To estimate the feasibility of N-CQDs-based fluorescence nanoprobe in river water and bovine serum samples, the standard spiked strategy was applied for the trace determination of diosmetin in the above samples. As exhibited in Table 1 , the range of recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD) were 94.8 %-108.8 % and lower than 3.44 %, suggesting the availability of the fluorescence platform established in the paper for the sensing of diosmetin in real samples, which provided a new project for the diosmetin determination.

The fluorescence change principle of N-CQDs for diosmetin detection was carried out. The transient fluorescence spectra were utilized to measure the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + diosmetin solution. The fluorescence lifetime spectra were displayed in Fig. S7A. The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + diosmetin were 2.40 ns and 2.36 ns through the calculation of relevant formulas, exhibiting an inconspicuous difference. Then, the dynamic quenching could be initially ruled out [49]. In Fig. S7B, the difference of UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs, diosmetin and N-CQDs + diosmetin were discussed. An absorption peak at 305 nm disappeared, which was ascribed to the generation of nonfluorescent groups. The above phenomenon was caused by the static quenching. Additionally, the excitation spectrum overlapped with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of diosmetin, which was an important reason for the inner filter effect (Fig. S7C) [50–53]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime also confirmed our hypothesis [54].

To determine diosmetin more handily and quickly, the visual determination of diosmetin were developed under UV light. The sensing colors were basically unchanged with diosmetin concentration increasing from 0 to 150 μM under sunlight (Fig. S8A). Meanwhile, the yellow fluorescence gradually varied from yellow to light green with diosmetin concentration increasing from 0 to 150 μM (Fig. S8B). The establishment of this system made it feasible to visually determine diosmetin more handily and quickly in sophisticated surroundings.

This scheme displayed the hydrothermal preparation of N-CQDs utilizing p-phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid as the precursors containing nitrogen and carbon. The N-CQDs held high fluorescence quantum yield, small particle size, excellent stability and yellow fluorescence (528 nm) under the excitation wavelength of 372 nm. When the diosmetin was dropped into the N-CQDs solution, the fluorescence response was ascribed to the static quenching and inner filter effect, and the fluorescence intensity was obviously quenched. The linear determination range and LOD were 0.5–150 μM and 0.049 μM, respectively. The recovery rates of river water and bovine serum samples affirmed the practicability and dependability of the as-developed strategy, and the potentiality the N-CQDs as fluorescence probes to determine diosmetin in real samples were verified. The establishment of this system could be visually determine diosmetin more handily and quickly in sophisticated surroundings.

Chang Tang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation. Rui Tong: Modification. Guoqing Deng: Software, Validation. Jing Yang: Visualization, Methodology. Yi Zhang: Software, Validation. Zhifeng Cai: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Hubei Natural Science Foundation Innovation and Development Joint Fund Project (2022CFD099), the Doctoral Startup Foundation of Hubei University of Arts and Science (No.2059135).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110362.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
